manifest.title: [super-qa] high: escape_like has zero tests despite 5+ production LIKE-clause call sites
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
escape_like() escapes backslash, percent, and underscore for SQLite LIKE clauses (ESCAPE '\\'). It is imported by conclusions.py:69, folder_summaries.py:27, bibtex.py:106, and papers.py:234 — every wildcard search in the codebase funnels through it. No test file references escape_like (confirmed: grep across tests/ returns 0 hits). The function is pure, order-sensitive (backslash MUST be escaped first or the %/_ escapes get double-escaped), and a regression here silently breaks search correctness or opens a LIKE-injection where a user keyword containing % matches everything. The ordering of the three .replace() calls is exactly the kind of subtle invariant that needs a guard test.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/db.py:54-59`

## Suggested approach
Add tests/test_db.py cases covering: no special chars (identity), a lone '%' -> '\\%', a lone '_' -> '\\_', a lone '\\' -> '\\\\', and a combined input like 'a_b%c\\d' to lock the backslash-first ordering. Optionally add a hypothesis property: for any string s, escaping then using it in a LIKE ... ESCAPE '\\' against the literal s matches exactly that row in an in-memory SQLite table (round-trip invariant).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Resolved at the cited location
- [ ] Regression test added (`uv run pytest`)
- [ ] `ruff check` + type check clean

## Metadata
- **Finding ID:** `db/testing-escape-like-untested` · **fingerprint:** `eafb0ba48107`
- **Category:** testing · **Area:** `area:db` · **Type:** `type:test` · **Auto-fixable:** no
- **Lens:** test · **References:** src/knowledge_base/conclusions.py:69, src/knowledge_base/folder_summaries.py:27, src/knowledge_base/bibtex.py:106, src/knowledge_base/papers.py:234

manifest.title: [super-qa] high: delete_chunk_vecs has no direct unit test for empty-input, chunk_count decrement
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
delete_chunk_vecs() is exported in __all__ and called from web.py (lines 843, 984, 1046) and indirectly via delete_chunks_cascade. No test references it directly (grep tests/ = 0). Three behaviours are uncovered: (1) the empty-list early return at line 458-459 (`if not chunk_ids: return`); (2) the chunk_count bookkeeping at 468-473 — it counts ACTUAL rows present before deleting (count_rows) and decrements embed_spaces.chunk_count with a MAX(0, ...) clamp, only when actual_deleted>0. Nothing asserts that count stays correct when some chunk_ids have no embedding, nor that the clamp prevents going negative; (3) the table_name override path. The batching boundary is exercised only indirectly through reingest (test_ingest.py:753 patches _SQL_BATCH_SIZE=2) but that test asserts reingest outcomes, not this function's chunk_count contract.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/db.py:449-473`

## Suggested approach
Add direct tests: (a) delete_chunk_vecs(conn, []) is a no-op and leaves chunk_count unchanged; (b) insert N vecs into the active space, delete a subset, assert embed_spaces.chunk_count decremented by exactly the number of rows that actually existed (mix in chunk_ids with no embedding to verify count uses actual rows); (c) verify MAX(0,...) clamp by deleting more than chunk_count and asserting count floors at 0; (d) delete from an explicit non-active table_name. Parametrize across element_type float32/int8.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Resolved at the cited location
- [ ] Regression test added (`uv run pytest`)
- [ ] `ruff check` + type check clean

## Metadata
- **Finding ID:** `db/testing-delete-chunk-vecs-untested` · **fingerprint:** `47498600266b`

## Problem
Current state: `_store_resolved` (lines 558-738, ~180 LOC inside one try/except) performs at least five distinct jobs: (1) clear prior extraction, (2) build surface->canonical lookup from resolution groups (568-579), (3) aggregate per-canonical entity_data via a defaultdict-of-dict (581-612), (4) insert into entities + entity_mentions (614-638), (5) write methods/datasets tables with a member-alias fan-out (640-690), (6) write metrics with canonical re-resolution (692-727). manifest.title: [super-qa] high: _extract_html_images is a ~215-LOC function mixing 5 responsibilities
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
Current state: `_extract_html_images` (lines 329-543, ~215 body LOC) parses candidates, merges extra HTML sources with dedup, downloads+size-guards+SSRF-rechecks each image, decodes via Pillow, calls vision per image, embeds, deletes stale chunks, and inserts new chunks with hash dedup — all in one function body. It far exceeds the 50-LOC threshold and operates at four different abstraction levels (byte streaming, image decoding, DB cleanup, embedding).
Target state: Extract cohesive helpers: `_download_image_bytes(url) -> bytes | None` (the httpx.stream + byte-cap + post-redirect SSRF block, lines 409-447), `_decode_image_to_png_b64(bytes) -> str | None` (lines 450-461), `_describe_image(b64, alt) -> list[(desc, meta)]` (lines 463-489), and a shared `_swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, index_start, index_end)` that owns the delete-stale + embed + insert-with-dedup tail (lines 494-543). The top-level function then orchestrates at one altitude.
Pattern name: Extract Method / Compose Method.
Motivation: Testability (each fallible stage independently mockable), readability, and it exposes the duplication shared with `_extract_web_figures`/`_extract_element_captures`.
Estimated scope: 1 file, ~6 new private helpers, plus test updates.
Prerequisites: none.
Risk: Medium — the embed-before-delete ordering (lines 494-516) is a deliberate crash-safety invariant; the extraction must preserve 'delete stale only after embeddings succeed' or it silently corrupts idempotency.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/web.py:329-543`

## Suggested approach
Decompose into _download_image_bytes / _decode_image_to_png_b64 / _describe_image / _swap_figure_chunks helpers; keep the embed-then-delete ordering intact in the shared swap helper.

manifest.title: [super-qa] high: Three figure-extraction functions duplicate the embed/delete-stale/insert-dedup
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
Current state: `_extract_html_images` (494-543), `_extract_web_figures` (829-881), and `_extract_element_captures` (969-1012) each repeat the identical pipeline: build `texts` list, call `_embed_with_config`, query stale `chunk_index` range for `source_type='figure'`, call `_cleanup_figure_fk_refs` + delete (via `delete_chunks_cascade` in one, `delete_chunk_vecs`+`_batched_execute` in the other two — an inconsistency, see separate finding), then loop `zip(collected, embeddings)` computing `_content_hash`, skip-if-exists, `_insert_chunk`, increment counter, `conn.commit()` if changed. Each also opens with `import base64` + `from .vision import _get_vision_config` + reads `base_url`/`model`.
Target state: A single `_swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, *, index_start, index_end=None)` that takes `collected: list[tuple[str, dict, int]]` (desc, meta, index-offset) and performs embed → scoped-delete → hash-dedup insert → commit. The three callers reduce to per-source collection logic only.
Pattern name: Form Template Method / Extract Superclass (here: extract shared procedure).
Motivation: ~120 lines of near-identical logic; the chunk-index-range scoping is subtle and currently copy-pasted, so a fix to one (e.g. the delete-API inconsistency) must be remembered in three places.
Estimated scope: 1 file, 1 new helper, 3 call-site rewrites, test consolidation.
Prerequisites: reconcile the delete-API inconsistency (delete_chunks_cascade vs delete_chunk_vecs+manual DELETE) first.
Risk: Medium — the index ranges differ per caller and encode an ordering contract between the three figure sources; must be parameterized exactly.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/web.py:494-543,829-881,969-1012`

## Suggested approach
Extract a parameterized _swap_figure_chunks(conn, source_url, collected, index_start, index_end) and route all three figure extractors through it.

manifest.title: [super-qa] high: _embed_with_config matryoshka base_dim branch never exercised
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
_embed_with_config has two branches gated on get_active_space(conn) returning a space with a 'matryoshka_base_dim' (lines 92-102). When base_dim is set, it embeds at base_dim then truncates every vector to cfg['dim'] via truncate_embedding, mapping None passthrough for zero-norm vectors. No test in tests/test_ingest.py creates an active space with matryoshka_base_dim, so this branch and the truncate-on-each-vector logic are completely uncovered in the ingest pipeline. truncate_embedding is unit-tested in isolation (tests/test_embeddings.py:280) but never integrated here. The only test that references _embed_with_config (test_embed_failure_does_not_leave_orphan_session_rows) patches it with a RuntimeError side_effect, so the real function body is never run with a configured space. A bug in the base_dim/truncate path (e.g. truncating to the wrong dim, or mishandling the None entry inside the list comprehension at line 101) would ship silently.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:90-108`

## Suggested approach
Add a test that sets an active embedding space with matryoshka_base_dim > cfg['dim'], patches knowledge_base.ingest.embed to return base_dim-length vectors (and one None), then calls _embed_with_config directly and asserts: (a) embed() was invoked with expected_dim == base_dim, (b) returned non-None vectors have len == cfg['dim'], (c) the None entry is preserved as None. Add a companion test for the non-matryoshka branch asserting expected_dim == cfg['dim'] and no truncation.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Resolved at the cited location
- [ ] Regression test added (`uv run pytest`)
- [ ] `ruff check` + type check clean

manifest.title: [super-qa] high: Reranker scores and RRF scores mixed on incomparable scales during final sort
manifest.body:
> Verified by multi-model debate (Gemini+Codex). See verdict below.

## Problem
When rerank=True, the cross-encoder is run only on rerank_candidates (capped at rerank_top_n, line 402-406) and only fetchable_cids get score_map[cid] = rs (line 418). Every other entry in score_map keeps its RRF score. reranker.rerank documents scores in [0, 1] (reranker.py:193), while RRF scores are 1/(RRF_K+rank+1) ≈ 0.016 at best and decreasing. The re-sort at line 422 sorts these two incompatible scales together. A reranked candidate that the cross-encoder judged a weak match (e.g. score 0.01, a legitimate value in [0,1]) is then sorted BELOW an un-reranked candidate whose RRF score is ~0.016 — even though that un-reranked candidate was excluded from reranking precisely because it ranked lower. This inverts the intended ordering: items the reranker explicitly scored can be pushed beneath items it never saw. The defect is masked whenever every reranked score happens to exceed the top RRF score (~0.016), so it surfaces intermittently on poor-match pools.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/search.py:402-422`

## Suggested approach
Do not mix scales. Either (a) restrict the final ranked list to only the reranked (scored) candidates when rerank succeeds — drop un-reranked entries from score_map before the re-sort — or (b) order primarily by a reranked/not-reranked tier (reranked items always above un-reranked tail), or (c) normalize RRF and reranker scores into a common scale before combining. Option (a)/(b) preserves the contract that reranker order wins.

## Debate verdict
- original **high** · gemini **high** · codex **medium** -> verdict **high**
- Opus HIGH: reranker[0,1] vs RRF(~0.016) co-sort; triggers at default top_k when source_type/chunk_strategy filter set; compounds with under-fetch; default unfiltered path unaffected (Codex caveat).

manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: Per-row embed_spaces.chunk_count UPDATE in insert_chunk_vec causes N+1 writes du
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
insert_chunk_vec() fires a second statement `UPDATE embed_spaces SET chunk_count = chunk_count + 1 WHERE table_name = ? AND status = 'active'` on every single call, right after the vec INSERT. The sole caller (src/knowledge_base/ingest.py:180) invokes insert_chunk_vec once per chunk inside the per-chunk ingestion path, so a document producing N chunks generates N redundant UPDATE statements against embed_spaces. Each UPDATE is a separate round-trip and a table scan/filter on (table_name, status). The chunk_count is pure bookkeeping derived from row inserts; it does not need to be incremented one-at-a-time. This is the classic N+1 write amplification pattern and is the dominant per-chunk overhead beyond the actual vector insert. The symmetric delete_chunk_vecs() already does a single batched count+decrement, showing the bulk-aware pattern is intended.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/db.py:442-446`

## Suggested approach
Decouple the count bookkeeping from the per-row insert: have insert_chunk_vec only do the vec INSERT, and add a separate `bump_chunk_count(conn, table_name, delta)` (or a bulk insert_chunk_vecs(...)) that the ingestion loop calls once after inserting all chunks for a document (delta = number inserted). Alternatively, drop the redundant counter entirely and compute chunk_count on demand via `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM [tbl]` when reporting status, since it is denormalized state that can drift. If keeping the per-call API for compatibility, add a `bump_count: bool = True` parameter so bulk callers can pass bump_count=False and update once.

`_record_entity` performs an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, then issues a second SELECT to retrieve the row id (lines 204-206). It is called once per method and once per dataset inside the per-entity loops of `_store_resolved` (lines 654-676) and `_extract_single_pass` (lines 781-835), doubling the statement count on the write path. The same INSERT-OR-IGNORE-then-fallback-SELECT shape recurs at lines 617-626, 793-805, and 822-835. SQLite supports `RETURNING` (since 3.35; CPython 3.12 bundles a newer SQLite), which collapses each pair into one statement.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/extraction.py:195-209`

## Suggested approach
Use `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(...) DO UPDATE SET ... RETURNING id` and read `.fetchone()` from the same cursor, eliminating the follow-up SELECT. For the `INSERT OR IGNORE` sites, `RETURNING id` returns the id on insert; keep a single fallback SELECT only for the conflict-skipped case. Confirm the minimum bundled SQLite version across target deployment platforms before adopting RETURNING unconditionally.

manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: N+1 per-chunk content_hash SELECT in ingest_url dedup loop
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
For every chunk of an ingested page, ingest_url issues a separate `SELECT id FROM chunks WHERE content_hash = ?` round-trip inside the for-loop. A long article can produce dozens-to-hundreds of chunks, so this is a classic N+1 query pattern: N synchronous SQLite calls where one batched query would suffice. content_hash is UNIQUE/indexed (db.py:189) so each query is cheap, but the per-call Python/SQLite overhead and round-trips dominate for large pages. The module already exposes `_batched_execute` (used elsewhere for IN-clause batching under the 999-var limit) — the existing-hash set could be fetched in one (or a few batched) `WHERE content_hash IN (...)` queries, then membership checked in-memory.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/web.py:1218-1228`

## Suggested approach
Collect all chunk hashes first, fetch existing rows in batched `SELECT id, content_hash FROM chunks WHERE content_hash IN ({ph})` calls via `_batched_execute`, build a {hash: id} dict, then iterate chunks checking the dict in memory instead of one query per chunk.

manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: SSRF: redirect-following reaches internal hosts before post-redirect validation
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
ingest_url validates the initial hostname against is_private_ip (web.py:1068-1078), but then calls httpx.get(url, follow_redirects=True, timeout=30.0) at web.py:1085. The post-redirect check at web.py:1091-1095 only blocks *processing* of the response; httpx has already followed every redirect hop and issued the actual HTTP request to whatever internal address a redirect pointed at. A public URL that 30x-redirects to http://169.254.169.254/ (cloud metadata) or http://127.0.0.1:port/ will have the request reach that internal endpoint, triggering any side effects (GET-as-action endpoints, SSRF-readable metadata) before the response host is rejected. The same pattern exists for inline image fetches at web.py:411-413 (httpx.stream with follow_redirects=True), with its post-redirect check at web.py:418. The code comment at web.py:1080-1083 explicitly acknowledges this gap and tracks the fix in issue #232. Being known-and-tracked does not remove it from an audit.

## Location
`web.py:1080-1095`

## Suggested approach
Implement per-hop SSRF validation. Disable automatic redirect following (follow_redirects=False) and manually walk redirects, running is_private_ip on each Location host before issuing the next request; or install a custom httpx transport that resolves the hostname, validates the resolved IP, and connects to that pinned IP. Apply the same per-hop guard to the image httpx.stream call at web.py:411-413.

manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: tmpdir lifecycle leaks across the function boundary instead of using a context m
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
`_render_with_browser` creates `tmpdir = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp(...))` (line 665), cleans it on every internal failure path (672, 693, 701) but on success returns it inside the result dict and documents 'Caller owns tmpdir cleanup on success' (663-664). The caller `ingest_url` must remember to `shutil.rmtree(tmpdir, ignore_errors=True)` in a finally (1173-1176). Split ownership across a function boundary is the exact problem context managers solve. Convert _render_with_browser into a contextmanager yielding a RenderResult and rmtree-ing the tmpdir on exit; callers use `with`. Removes the manual finally in ingest_url.

manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: source_type filtered only at final SQL fetch — can return fewer than top_k resul
manifest.body:
> Verified by multi-model debate (Gemini+Codex). See verdict below.

## Problem
Unlike chunk_strategy, which is pre-filtered against the candidate pool before RRF/boost/rerank (line 327), source_type is applied only at the terminal SQL fetch. chunk_ids = ranked[:top_k] (line 430) selects the top_k candidates WITHOUT regard to source_type; the SQL at lines 446-450 then drops any rows whose source_type does not match (" AND source_type = ?"). The build loop (lines 457-471) skips cids missing from chunk_map. Net effect: when source_type is supplied, the function can return fewer than top_k results even when enough matching candidates existed deeper in the merged/ranked list. The 3x over-fetch (line 254) and 2x boost window (line 363) only soften the shortfall; they do not eliminate it (e.g. a query where the relevant source_type is sparse among the top_k by score). The asymmetry with chunk_strategy (which IS pre-filtered) is the tell that this is an oversight, not a deliberate design choice.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/search.py:430-450`

## Suggested approach
Pre-filter the candidate pool by source_type the same way chunk_strategy is handled: either fold source_type into the pre-RRF filter block (lines 326-342) by adding it to the chunks JOIN/SELECT, or apply it when selecting chunk_ids so that ranked[:top_k] already contains only source_type-matching candidates. Then top_k matching results are returned whenever they exist in the over-fetched pool.

## Debate verdict
- original **high** · gemini **medium** · codex **medium** -> verdict **medium**
- Both models MEDIUM: source_type applied only at final SQL fetch; 3x overfetch softens but architecturally inconsistent with chunk_strategy pre-filter.

## Acceptance criteria

> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
Current state: the figure object flows through the pipeline as a plain dict and accumulates side-channel keys by mutation: _source_image (1799), _vision_failed (1819), _region_idx (1830) in _dispatch_vision_calls; _ocr_text via {**fig, '_ocr_text': ...} in _enrich_with_omniparser (1876, 1893); _assembled_content and _caption in _persist_figures (1939-1940). Motivation: typed fields catch key typos, document the contract, and stop the 'string key as schema' pattern; mypy/pyright can then verify the pipeline. manifest.title: [super-qa] medium: estimate_figures_time docstring claims an {"error": ...} return that the functio
manifest.body:
> Generated by `/super-qa`, agent analysis — review before action (not source-verified).

## Problem
The docstring states 'Returns {"error": ...} on validation failure.' (line 2100). The function body has no try/except: validation is delegated to _resolve_paper_context (raises NotFoundError) and _validate_pages (raises ValidationError) at lines 2102-2103, and those exceptions propagate uncaught. On the success path the function returns a dict with keys extracted_images/vector_pages/mixed_vector_regions/estimated_seconds/has_omniparser/omniparser_server_healthy (lines 2128-2135) — never an 'error' key. The {"error": ...} dict is actually produced by the route wrapper in src/knowledge_base/routes/extraction.py:245-247, which catches KnowledgeBaseError. The docstring misattributes the wrapper's error-handling contract to this function; a caller relying on it (checking for an 'error' key instead of catching the exception) would crash on validation failure.

## Location
`src/knowledge_base/vision.py:2090-2135`

## Suggested approach
Replace the line 'Returns {"error": ...} on validation failure.' with an accurate Returns/Raises description: the function returns the ETA dict on success and raises NotFoundError / ValidationError on invalid paper_id or page bounds (which the MCP route wrapper converts to an error dict).
